items.misconception: -1, by computing 5 - 2 x 3 and ignoring the negative.
items.why: Students drop the negative sign during substitution or fail to use brackets, leading to sign errors.

items.correct: 4.6 — look at the second decimal (4), which is less than 5, so round down.
items.misconception: 4.7, because the 8 at the end rounds the 4 up to 5, which then rounds the 6 up to 7.
items.random_wrong: 4.65, because rounding to 1 dp means cutting after the second decimal.
items.why: Students apply 'rounding up' digit-by-digit from the right (chained rounding), instead of looking only at the next digit after the place to which they are rounding.

items.correct: 9 — squaring a negative gives a positive.
items.misconception: (-3)^2 equals -9 because the minus is preserved.
items.random_wrong: (-3)^2 equals -6 because the negative makes you subtract.
items.why: Students confuse (-3)^2 with -3^2; without brackets the second is indeed -9, but with brackets the negative is squared too.
The answer examiners want is: reject H₀. “accept H₀” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: rejecting H₀ when it is true
items.misconception: accepting H₀ when it is false
items.why: Correct answer: rejecting H₀ when it is true. “accepting H₀ when it is false” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: At α = 5%, what is the critical p-value threshold (give as a decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
